worth_it_explanation: Yahoo DSP's Inventory Quality Targeting section on the Line consolidates the platform's brand safety mechanisms in one place: bot avoidance, viewability thresholds, contextual category controls, and App/Site lists. These controls exist in the UI and take effect only when they are actively configured. A Line with no contextual exclusions applied, no App/Site blocklist in place, and safety settings that vary from one Line to the next will deliver impressions in content environments the brand may not have approved. These checks are fast to run inside the Yahoo DSP interface and require no external tooling beyond the practitioner's own access.
intro: Yahoo DSP surfaces its brand safety controls inside the Inventory Quality Targeting section on each Line. Three mechanisms are available at that level: bot avoidance, which blocks known fraud and malware placements; viewability targeting, which restricts delivery to inventory meeting a defined viewability threshold; and contextuals, which use Yahoo DSP's content-category taxonomy to target or avoid specific content environments. A separate App/Site list control allows advertisers to apply domain and app allowlists or blocklists at the Line level. These checks confirm that contextual exclusions have been applied to avoid brand-unsafe content categories, that App/Site lists are used to reinforce contextual controls where appropriate, and that brand safety settings are applied consistently across Lines rather than left at permissive defaults on some and configured on others.

audit_checks.title: No contextual category exclusions are applied, so ads can serve next to brand-unsafe content
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Yahoo DSP's contextuals allow advertisers to block specific content-category contexts before the bid. Without any category exclusions applied, Lines bid on all content types Yahoo DSP's exchange makes available, including categories such as alcohol, drugs, and violence that many brands explicitly want to avoid. The gap is pre-bid: no impression-level filtering occurs after the fact to compensate for a missing category exclusion.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP's Inventory Quality Targeting section on each Line includes a Contextuals control. A contextual is a content-category classification that Yahoo DSP uses to describe the environment surrounding an ad placement. The taxonomy covers broad interest areas such as Arts and Entertainment, Automotive, and Home and Garden, but it also covers categories intended for brand safety avoidance, including Alcohol, Drugs, and Firearms. Advertisers can target a contextual to reach a specific content type, or block it to prevent their ads from serving in that context.

When no contextual category exclusions are applied to a Line, the Line bids on all content types the exchange classifies, including the avoidance categories. The control is applied at the pre-bid stage, so once an impression is served, no remediation can reverse the adjacency. The contextuals also include quality-oriented categories covering contexts such as ad density and page quality, which allow advertisers to layer inventory quality filtering on top of content-category controls.

The Yahoo Buying Guide on Microsoft Learn confirms that Contextuals are part of Inventory Quality Targeting on the Line and describes them as targeting or blocking specific categories of interest to advertisers, including brand safety avoidance contexts. Yahoo DSP's contextual taxonomy is also aligned with the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy, the industry standard for content classification in brand safety and contextual targeting workflows.

A few signs point to contextual exclusions being absent:
  • The Inventory Quality Targeting section on a Line shows no contextual categories selected for blocking
  • The Line targets all exchanges in open market with no App/Site list or contextual filter narrowing the eligible inventory pool
  • The account has no documented brand safety brief specifying which content categories the brand wants to avoid
You may want to request a brand safety brief or brand suitability policy from the client before selecting exclusion categories, so the configuration reflects the brand's actual requirements rather than a generic default.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, the contextual avoidance categories most worth reviewing are those associated with negative brand associations: content about product recalls, dangerous goods, or consumer financial distress. You may want to confirm whether the brand sells age-restricted products. That detail affects which avoidance categories take priority.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, content-environment credibility can affect form completion intent. You may want to consider whether the avoidance categories covering sensationalist or low-quality content environments are applied, particularly for campaigns where the offer depends on the visitor's trust in the surrounding context.

learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: Inventory Quality Targeting

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's contextuals documentation (help.yahooinc.com/dsp) is available to logged-in users. Contact your Yahoo DSP account team or check the Help Center under Inventory Quality Targeting for the full list of available contextual categories and their definitions.

investigate.intro: Open each active Line in Yahoo DSP and review the Inventory Quality Targeting section to confirm whether contextual category exclusions are applied.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: In Yahoo DSP, navigate to the Advertisers view and select the advertiser you are auditing.
From the Campaigns page, select the campaign containing the Lines you want to review.
On the Lines list view, select an active Line to open its settings.
Navigate to the Targeting section within the Line and locate the Inventory Quality Targeting area.

Within Inventory Quality Targeting, find the Contextuals control. Note whether any content categories are selected for blocking. If the control shows no categories selected or is set to its default state, record that no contextual exclusions are applied.
Repeat for two or three additional active Lines, particularly any serving in open exchange without a private marketplace deal or App/Site allowlist restricting the inventory pool.
Note the name of each Line reviewed, whether contextual exclusions exist, and which categories are blocked or allowed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether contextual category exclusions are applied to each sampled Line. A Line that blocks at least the content-category avoidance contexts documented in the brand's safety brief passes. A Line with no contextual exclusions applied, or a Line where the only brand safety control is bot avoidance with no content-category filter, is a fail recorded with the Line name and the categories that are absent.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Inventory Quality Targeting Contextuals settings for each sampled Line above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Yahoo DSP Lines reviewed for {{site_name}} have no contextual category exclusions applied in the Inventory Quality Targeting section. Without content-category exclusions, each Line bids on all content types Yahoo DSP's exchange makes available, including avoidance categories such as alcohol, drugs, and other contexts the brand may want to avoid. The gap is pre-bid, so no impression-level filter compensates after the fact. {{voice_recommend}} obtaining the brand's documented content-category exclusion list, applying the appropriate avoidance categories to the Contextuals control on each Line, and confirming that the selections align with the brand's current safety brief rather than a one-time configuration that is never revisited.

audit_checks.title: High-sensitivity Lines rely on a blocklist without a corresponding allowlist, leaving protection dependent on an incomplete domain list
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A blocklist identifies specific domains and apps to exclude, but it can only block what has already been added to the list. New domains, relaunched properties, and user-generated content subdomains that are not yet in the list continue to receive bids. For high-sensitivity campaigns where brand adjacency is closely scrutinized, an allowlist that restricts delivery to a pre-approved inventory set provides a tighter guarantee than a blocklist that attempts to enumerate every unsafe placement.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP's App/Site list feature, documented in the Inventory Quality Targeting section of the Yahoo Buying Guide on Microsoft Learn, allows advertisers to create and apply allowlists or blocklists at the Line level. Each list contains domains, apps, or app bundle IDs. An allowlist restricts delivery to only the inventory included in the list. A blocklist excludes the inventory in the list but allows everything else.

Both list types serve a purpose, but they provide different guarantees. A blocklist protects against known bad placements. It cannot protect against unknown ones: domains that launch after the list was last updated, properties that migrate to a new domain, or subdomains of user-generated content platforms that carry variable content quality. An allowlist inverts the logic. Instead of blocking known bad inventory, it permits only known good inventory. For a campaign where brand adjacency will be closely reviewed by the client, this distinction matters.

App/Site lists in Yahoo DSP follow the same domain and app bundle mechanics described in the Xandr Invest Inventory Lists documentation: lists can contain domains, subdomains, and app bundle IDs, and they can be scoped to a specific advertiser or applied more broadly.

A few signs point to a blocklist-only posture on a high-sensitivity Line:
  • The Line's App/Site list shows a blocklist but no corresponding allowlist
  • The Line is running in open exchange with no private marketplace deal providing a curated inventory pool
  • The campaign context is high-sensitivity: a pharmaceutical, financial services, or children's product where content adjacency carries regulatory or reputational risk
You may want to assess whether the campaign's placement requirements are tight enough to benefit from an allowlist before building one. An overly narrow allowlist can limit scale in ways that affect delivery.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a product launch or brand campaign where placement quality will be part of the post-campaign report, you may want to consider whether the blocklist covers the inventory categories the brand wants to avoid or whether an allowlist of pre-approved publisher domains would provide more defensible protection.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser where domain-level performance varies, you may want to consider building the allowlist from domains that have historically delivered acceptable conversion rates rather than starting from a brand safety brief. Both quality signals and brand safety requirements can be addressed through the same list.

learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: App/Site List Targeting

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's App/Site list creation and management documentation is available to logged-in users at help.yahooinc.com/dsp. Contact your Yahoo DSP account team for details on how inventory lists are scoped and applied across advertisers and campaigns.

investigate.intro: Review the App/Site list settings on Lines serving high-sensitivity campaigns and confirm whether the campaign's brand safety posture calls for an allowlist.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: In Yahoo DSP, navigate to the advertiser and open the campaign containing the Lines you want to review.

For each Line running a high-sensitivity campaign (pharmaceutical, financial, children's product, or any campaign where placement quality will be reviewed by the client), open the Line's Targeting settings.
Within the Inventory Quality Targeting or the App/Site list section, note whether the Line has an allowlist applied, a blocklist applied, or neither.
If only a blocklist is present, review the list to confirm it reflects a recently maintained set of exclusions rather than a placeholder or an original list that has not been updated.
Note the number of domains and apps in the blocklist. A very short list (fewer than a few hundred entries) on a Line with no contextual exclusions may indicate that brand safety controls are minimal.
Record each Line's name, whether an allowlist or blocklist is applied, and the approximate size and last-updated date of any list in place.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner identifies Lines running high-sensitivity campaigns and records whether those Lines use an allowlist, a blocklist, or neither. A high-sensitivity Line with an allowlist applied passes. A high-sensitivity Line with only a blocklist passes if the blocklist is recent and reflects the brand's documented exclusion requirements, but this should be flagged as a recommendation to consider whether an allowlist would provide stronger protection. A high-sensitivity Line with neither list type applied is a fail recorded with the Line name and the campaign context.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the App/Site list settings for each high-sensitivity Line above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that high-sensitivity Lines in {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP account rely on a domain blocklist without a corresponding allowlist, leaving brand safety dependent on a list that can only exclude what has already been added to it. New or relaunched domains, and user-generated content properties not yet in the blocklist, continue to receive bids from these Lines. {{voice_recommend}} evaluating whether the placement requirements for each high-sensitivity campaign are tight enough to support an allowlist of pre-approved publisher domains and apps, replacing the blocklist-only posture for those Lines with an allowlist that restricts delivery to inventory the brand has explicitly approved.

audit_checks.title: Brand safety settings are applied inconsistently across Lines, leaving gaps in some campaigns
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When some Lines in an account have contextual exclusions and App/Site lists applied and others do not, spend from the less-protected Lines exposes the brand even when the overall account appears to have brand safety in place. The inconsistency is hard to detect from a campaign-level view because the account-level summary does not surface per-Line control gaps.

educate.base:
Yahoo DSP applies brand safety controls at the Line level, not at the advertiser or campaign level. Each Line has its own Inventory Quality Targeting section where bot avoidance, viewability, contextual category controls, and App/Site list settings are configured. There is no single advertiser-level brand safety default that all Lines inherit automatically.

This architecture means that a new Line added to an existing campaign will not inherit the safety configuration of its sibling Lines. The practitioner setting up the Line must apply contextual exclusions, ads.txt compliance, and any App/Site lists explicitly. When Line setup is handled under time pressure, across multiple team members, or without a written checklist, it is straightforward for some Lines to end up with safety controls and others to end up without them.

The Yahoo Buying Guide on Microsoft Learn confirms that Inventory Quality Targeting (bot avoidance, viewability, contextuals, and App/Site list) is configured individually on the Line. There is no advertiser-level template that pre-populates these settings.

A few signs point to inconsistent application:
  • Lines within the same campaign or insertion order carry different contextual exclusion configurations with no documented reason for the difference
  • Some Lines have an App/Site list applied and others serving the same audience and creative do not
  • Newer Lines added to long-running campaigns show fewer safety controls than the original Lines in that campaign
You may want to build a simple per-Line inventory of brand safety settings before deciding what to change, so that the audit identifies gaps rather than producing changes without a clear before-state recorded.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running concurrent prospecting and retargeting Lines, the brand safety requirements for both Line types may differ. Prospecting Lines reach a broader inventory pool and may benefit from stricter contextual exclusions. You may want to confirm that differences between Lines are deliberate and documented rather than the result of setup drift.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser with Lines organized by product, geography, or audience segment, different Lines may legitimately carry different settings. You may want to confirm that any differences reflect a deliberate strategy and that the Lines handling the most brand-sensitive audiences are not the ones with the weakest controls.

learn_more.source_title: Yahoo Buying Guide: Inventory Quality Targeting

educate.learn_more_note: Yahoo DSP's documentation on Line-level brand safety configuration and how to copy settings between Lines is available to logged-in users at help.yahooinc.com/dsp. Contact your Yahoo DSP account team for details on whether bulk Line editing is available for brand safety settings.

investigate.intro: Survey the Inventory Quality Targeting settings across a representative sample of active Lines to identify where settings diverge.

yahoo_dsp_ui.label: Yahoo DSP UI
steps: In Yahoo DSP, open the advertiser and navigate to the Campaigns view. Select a campaign with multiple active Lines.

For each of four to six active Lines, open the Line's Targeting settings and note the state of the Inventory Quality Targeting section: whether bot avoidance is enabled, whether viewability targeting is applied, whether any contextual categories are blocked, and whether an App/Site list is attached.
Record the settings for each Line in a simple table: Line name, bot avoidance on or off, contextual exclusions applied or absent, App/Site list applied or absent.

Identify Lines where the settings diverge from what the majority of Lines in that campaign carry. Note whether any of the more permissive Lines are serving the same audience or creative as Lines with stronger controls.
If any Lines show fewer controls than their siblings with no documented rationale, record those Lines by name for the evaluation.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the Inventory Quality Targeting configuration for each sampled Line and identifies where settings diverge within the same campaign. Lines with the same audience and creative targets that carry different safety configurations are flagged. Divergence that is documented and maps to a deliberate campaign decision passes. Undocumented divergence, where some Lines carry contextual exclusions and others do not with no recorded rationale, is a fail recorded with the Line names and the specific settings that differ.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Inventory Quality Targeting settings for each sampled Line above to compare them against sibling Lines in the same campaign.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that brand safety settings are applied inconsistently across {{site_name}}'s Yahoo DSP Lines. Some Lines have contextual category exclusions and App/Site lists configured, while others serving similar audiences carry neither control. Because Yahoo DSP applies Inventory Quality Targeting at the Line level with no advertiser-wide default, Lines added without a safety configuration checklist may be bidding on content environments the brand has not approved. {{voice_recommend}} auditing all active Lines against a documented brand safety checklist, applying contextual exclusions and App/Site lists uniformly to Lines that share the same audience and creative, and establishing a Line setup process that includes brand safety configuration as a required step rather than an optional one.
